source.title: Curiosity-driven Exploration by Self-supervised Prediction
source.venue: Proceedings of the 34th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2017)

confidence_rationale: Foundational paper directly instantiating MECH-314c (learning-progress curiosity via forward-model PE). High source quality (ICML, highly cited). Mapping fidelity is moderate: the REE substrate uses e3._running_variance as the PE proxy rather than a dedicated learned feature space, which is Phase-1 approximation. The stochastic environment failure mode Pathak 2017 identifies is directly REE-relevant: a random causal grid world with intrinsic novelty only could exhibit the same failure unless the environment has structure the agent can learn to predict.
failure_signatures: In stochastic environments prediction error stays high even after optimal learning, producing an endlessly high curiosity signal that does not track real learning progress -- the environment randomness is indistinguishable from learnable structure.
When the learned feature space is poor, prediction error measures noise rather than meaningful state transitions, misdirecting exploration.
Curiosity alone (without any task reward) is insufficient to solve tasks requiring goal-directed persistence -- it drives coverage, not completion.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Curiosity formulated as the error in an agent's ability to predict the consequence of its own actions in a learned visual feature space provides a useful intrinsic reward for exploration in sparse-reward and no-reward settings.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-314c uses EMA of |PE_t - PE_{t-K}| (learning-progress signal derived from e3._running_variance) as the broadcast scalar sub-flavour of the structured-curiosity bonus. This is the Phase-1 instantiation of the Pathak intrinsic-reward tradition: the agent should prefer actions whose consequences it is currently improving at predicting.
mapping.mapping_caveat: REE Phase 1 uses a broadcast scalar (global running variance) rather than per-candidate forward-model error. This means all K candidates receive the same MECH-314c nudge regardless of which candidate would produce more informative next states -- the per-candidate refinement is explicitly deferred to Phase 2. Pathak's architecture uses a learned inverse dynamics model to filter out environmentally uncontrollable factors; REE Phase 1 lacks this and is vulnerable to the stochastic-environment failure if the CausalGridWorld environment has irreducible stochastic components.
mapping.source_context: Simulated robotics environments: VizDoom first-person 3D navigation and Super Mario Bros platformer. Both are relatively structured (the agent's actions have predictable physical consequences); the stochastic failure is noted as a theoretical concern and demonstrated in a 3D task where the agent controls part of the environment.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high: ICML 2017, over 3000 citations, foundational reference for the field. Mapping fidelity reduced because REE Phase 1 uses a broadcast scalar proxy rather than per-candidate learned prediction error (architectural mismatch). Transfer risk is moderate: the core insight (PE as curiosity) is domain-general, but the specific stochastic failure condition depends on CausalGridWorld's stochasticity profile.
